text: The video shows a large abdominal wall defect, visibly prominent in the surgical field.
A 5mm VisiPort is inserted on the left side at the level of the umbilicus to establish pneumoperitoneum, preparing the abdominal cavity for laparoscopic access.

Dense honeycomb adhesions are encountered upon entry, and the surgeon uses the camera to gently establish a space for inserting a second working port, followed by the commencement of adhesion division.
The surgeon performs extensive division of adhesions using both blunt and sharp dissection techniques, carefully avoiding the central hernia defect to re-establish normal anatomical structures.

Additional working ports are inserted, and after extensive division of adhesions, the anatomy becomes clearer. The camera provides a view from the left side towards the splenic flexure, revealing the extensive midline hernia and the left colon.
The surgeon visualizes the descending colon and begins its mobilization, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The patient is positioned with the left side elevated, and multiple working ports are visibly placed from the left upper quadrant to the left lower quadrant of the abdomen.
The surgeon begins lateral mobilization of the left colon, facing challenges due to the port placement directly above the working area.
The surgeon continues lateral mobilization of the left colon up to the splenic flexure, dividing dense medial omental adhesions using dissection techniques.

At the pelvic brim, the surgeon encounters extensive inflammatory adhesions, which are non-anatomical and likely a result of previous diverticulitis. These adhesions obscure the normal anatomical structures and require careful dissection.
The surgeon carefully dissects the descending colon away from retroperitoneal structures, ensuring separation without damaging surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects at the pelvic brim, taking care to avoid retroperitoneal structures like the ureter, which may be affected by previous inflammation.

In the pelvis, the surgeon carefully dissects extensive small bowel adhesions from the sigmoid colon and its mesentery to facilitate medial vascular dissection and high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery.
With space established, the surgeon begins the medial dissection of the inferior mesenteric artery, carefully navigating the surgical field to avoid surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs skeletonization of the vascular pedicle, carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue despite the challenging port positioning.
The surgeon uses an energy device to assist in the precise skeletonization of the vascular pedicle, ensuring accurate dissection and separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully identifies and dissects retroperitoneal structures away from the sigmoid colon, preparing for a left lower quadrant incision to extract the specimen.
The surgeon resects the colon en bloc along with adherent small bowel, visually indicating an association with local tumor perforation.
The surgeon performs a primary stapled colorectal anastomosis and a side-to-side stapled small bowel anastomosis, completing the procedure without the need for a defunctioning ileostomy.
